entries.title: The Murmuration
entries.description: Each autumn evening over the marshes of the Somerset Levels, hundreds of thousands of starlings gather to perform their hypnotic aerial ballet — swooping and surging in perfect unison — before dropping into the reeds to roost.

entries.title: Bioluminescent Bay, Puerto Rico
entries.description: In Mosquito Bay on the island of Vieques, single-celled dinoflagellates flash brilliant blue-green light when disturbed. On moonless nights, every paddle stroke, every ripple, and every fish trails a cold blue fire through the water.

entries.title: Painted Hills, Oregon
entries.description: In the John Day Fossil Beds, millions of years of volcanic ash and ancient soils have been exposed by erosion into layered bands of gold, red, and black. The hills change color with the light and the moisture in the air.

entries.title: Spring Ephemeral: Trout Lily
entries.description: For just a few fleeting weeks each April, the forest floor comes alive with trout lilies — their mottled, trout-speckled leaves and nodding yellow flowers emerging before the canopy leafs out and shuts away the light.

entries.title: Mount Rainier Wildflower Meadows
entries.description: At 5,400 feet on the slopes of Washington's greatest volcano, Paradise Meadows bursts into a riot of color each July — Indian paintbrush, avalanche lilies, and lupine stretching toward the eternal glaciers above.

entries.title: The Great Banyan, India
entries.description: In the botanical garden of Howrah, a single banyan tree has sent down so many aerial roots that it now spans 3.5 acres and appears to be a forest. Planted around 1750, it is thought to be the world's widest tree.

entries.title: Kelp Forest, Channel Islands
entries.description: Giant kelp off the California coast grows up to two feet per day, creating cathedral-like underwater cathedrals 150 feet tall. Sea otters, garibaldi fish, and leopard sharks weave through green-gold light filtering from the surface.
